title: Use a new catatonit per namespace set
body:
### Issue Description

With multiple namespaces, the persistent `catatonit` persists the selection that it was started with. When there is a namespace mismatch, a new `catatonit` process should be used.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Start a new namespace (e.g., networking)
2. `podman run` in one namespace
3. `podman run` in the other inherits the other's namespace and cannot use networking properly.


### Describe the results you received

Container cannot contact the network when running `podman` from a new network namespace.

### Describe the results you expected

The namespace selection of `podman` itself should be used for containers (except those that `podman` creates for its own operations).

### podman info output

comments.body:
It is not clear what you are asking for, can please provide a full reproducer.

Generally speaking spawning podman from more than one namespace will cause major consistency issues and a lot weird corner cases so the answer should likely be no that is not something that we ever can support.

I have a wireguard VPN setup. I push it into a namespace. However, if I want to access VPN-only resources via a container, the `catatonit -P` ends up using the original namespace and cannot access the resources. It works if I kill `catatonit -P` and launch `podman` from within the namespace. I'm not sure how to provide a full reproducer as it involves setting up a Wireguard network. See [this page](https://www.wireguard.com/netns/) for instructions. Once you have it set up:

```sh
# Start a `tmux` in each network namespace
sudo ip netns exec physical su $USER -c 'tmux -L physical start-server'
sudo ip netns exec wireguard su $USER -c 'tmux -L wireguard start-server'

# Launch a container in the physical namespace
tmux -L physical -N
echo "in tmux" && podman run --rm curlimages/curl curl ddg.gg
tmux detach-session

# Run in the wireguard namespace
tmux -L wireguard -N
# This fails because the container ends up in the `physical` namespace due to the leftover `catatonit -P` process
echo "in tmux" && podman run --rm curlimages/curl curl some-wg-only-resource
```

Well at the very least show us how you create the container.

catatonit is only used to hold the user + mnt namespace, it is not used to switch the netns and we cannot switch the netns to another netns not created within the podman user namespace because we would have no perms to switch to either physical or wireguard so we just run from the current one.

If I run a container first to create the userns in the normal host namspaces and then run something like
```
sudo unshare -n su - $USER -c "podman run --rm quay.io/libpod/testimage:20240123 ping -c 1 google.com"
```
it correctly fails as the slirp4netns process for this container would be spawned in the new netns which does not have any connectivity.

This may have a different behaviour if you use `--network bridge` as this only ever uses one slirp4netns process so it would use the first container that was started.

> Well at the very least show us how you create the container.

That I did show; they're normal images with plain `podman run`…though I see I did `exec` here :facepalm: . Edited.

> catatonit is only used to hold the user + mnt namespace

Ah! This might be the core of the issue here. It looks like `ip netns` [sets up *mount* namespaces for `/sys`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45629002/how-does-ip-netns-exec-command-create-mount-namespace). But it doesn't set up the *child* mounts (I've had to fix `/sys/fs/cgroup` in the namespaces before). So it gets put into the network namespace, but the `/sys` tree is mismatched.

I don't think this is something we can support.

If you want to run podman from different network namespaces, you need to use a different `--root` for each, so they don't step on each other.

> If you want to run podman from different network namespaces, you need to use a different --root for each, so they don't step on each other.

I doubt that this makes any difference we still only ever use one pause process regardless of --root.

A `--fresh-pause` flag (though rename as needed) would be sufficient for my needs. At least I'm fine with a "I know I need this" rather than magic heuristics. Making it take an argument like `yes` and `no` could allow some level of `=auto` in the future if it ever comes to it.

> A --fresh-pause flag (though rename as needed) would be sufficient for my needs. At least I'm fine with a "I know I need this" rather than magic heuristics. Making it take an argument like yes and no could allow some level of =auto in the future if it ever comes to it.

That is not an option for us, there is only ever one pause process. Creating a second one will cause a lot of havoc that is simple not supportable for us. If container a runs in userns a and then you call podman stop a from userns b it would fail...

> > If you want to run podman from different network namespaces, you need to use a different --root for each, so they don't step on each other.
> 
> I doubt that this makes any difference we still only ever use one pause process regardless of --root.

the network namespaces can be owned by the same user namespace, so they can share the same pause process.

If it is not enough, then it needs a different `--runroot` as well, so it uses a different pause process.
FWIW, my current "workaround" is to kill the `catatonit -P` process and then run from the network namespace. What dragons am I poking with a stick by doing that?

> A `--fresh-pause` flag (though rename as needed) would be sufficient for my needs. At least I'm fine with a "I know I need this" rather than magic heuristics. Making it take an argument like `yes` and `no` could allow some level of `=auto` in the future if it ever comes to it.

that defeats having a pause process at all.  We must make sure all processes are owned by the same user namespace, a different pause process will be owned by a different user namespace unless you make sure to use `podman unshare` first.
  
The `podman unshare` command, and the pause process, is the equivalent of running as root, once there you do all your manual tweaks.

You can no longer properly manage any containers started in the previous userns.
All the unmounts, etc.. will fail and cause a lot of errrors. Also note that just killing the pause pid is not enough if there are still running container we try to join the userns back via the conmon pid if possible.

If you never have any running containers when you do this you should be mostly be fine, but again this is not something we will support.
Also would it make more sense for you to use `nsenter -n` instead because this will not change the mountns.

> Also would it make more sense for you to use nsenter -n instead because this will not change the mountns.

Thanks; I'll try this out.

It seems that some mount namespace is needed to interpose `/etc/resolv.conf` to use the DNS settings for the wireguard tunnel. I'll see about getting `ip netns exec` to mount sub-`/sys` mounts in its mount namespace again at least (then `podman` can run containers even if networking is busted).
I am closing this issue for now, since it is not something we want to support in Podman.  Feel free to comment further and keep the conversion going if you've more questions

> If it is not enough, then it needs a different --runroot as well, so it uses a different pause process.

I tried with `--root=… --runroot=` and it still seems to have reused the existing `catatonit` process.
Yes we only ever use one pause process regardless of --root/--runroot
can you try overriding XDG_RUNTIME_DIR?

Ok, that managed to make it use another pause process. So it looks like the steps to make this work are:

- make the network namespace
- remount `cgroup2` in it at `/sys/fs/cgroup`
- make `podman` use a separate storage and run root
  - use `CONTAINERS_STORAGE_CONF` and `CONTAINERS_CONF` to relocate configuration files
